figures.gloss: The version most of the world knows — from Carlos Castaneda's Don Juan books. Richard de Mille documented it as fabrication: no Yaqui words in books about a Yaqui sorcerer, library records placing Castaneda at UCLA during his 'desert fieldwork', a 47-page glossary of plagiarised sources.
figures.facet: The forged shard, kept in deliberately so the real seam can be told from the fake. Its line points home to the grounded Toltec lineage it imitated.
figures.provenance: de Mille, The Don Juan Papers (1980); 'the greatest anthropological hoax since Piltdown Man'.

date.note: The fabricated shard, datable to publication.

edges.note: The famous version is fabricated. Its seam is aimed deliberately back at the grounded Toltec line it imitated — the fake set beside the real so the difference can be seen.

dossier.essence: Not an ethnic tradition but a literary fabrication that functioned as one: Carlos Castaneda's Don Juan books (1968–1998) presented themselves as UCLA anthropological fieldwork recording an apprenticeship to a Yaqui sorcerer, don Juan Matus. The 'fieldwork' earned Castaneda a doctorate (1973) and the books sold in the millions, seeding a real following that outlived their exposure. Richard de Mille's source criticism (1976, 1980) documented the forgery: internal chronologies contradicted by library records placing Castaneda at UCLA during his claimed desert fieldwork, an absence of genuinely Yaqui content, and passages traceable in bulk to prior published sources.
dossier.cosmos: As doctrine, the books teach that ordinary reality is only a learned 'description': the tonal (the ordered island of the known) floats within the nagual (the unknowable), and the sorcerer shifts worlds by moving the 'assemblage point' of perception. In the later books, awareness is food for the Eagle at death, and the warrior's aim is to burn 'with the fire from within' and slip past with consciousness intact. The system is a bricolage of Mesoamerican vocabulary, Asian and Western mysticism, phenomenology, and 1960s psychedelia rather than any attested Yaqui cosmology.
dossier.practice: As promulgated: 'stopping the world,' erasing personal history, 'recapitulation' of one's life, controlled dreaming, and — in the early books only — psychotropic allies (peyote, datura, mushroom smoke), later demoted to training aids. In the 1990s Castaneda's inner circle marketed Tensegrity, choreographed 'magical passes,' through the Cleargreen corporation. The costs were real: followers organized their lives around a fictitious lineage, and several women of the inner circle vanished immediately after Castaneda's 1998 death — the remains of one, Patricia Partin, were later found in Death Valley.
dossier.canon: Twelve books, 'The Teachings of Don Juan: A Yaqui Way of Knowledge' (1968) through 'The Active Side of Infinity' (1998); the counter-canon is Richard de Mille's 'Castaneda's Journey' (1976) and 'The Don Juan Papers' (1980).
dossier.reflects: The appetite for credentialed exotic wisdom — a fabricated ethnography functioned as scripture for decades, showing that claims of authentication, not content alone, can power a tradition; kept in the atlas as the fake shard against which real seams are measured.
sources: Richard de Mille, 'Castaneda's Journey: The Power and the Allegory' (1976)
Richard de Mille (ed.), 'The Don Juan Papers: Further Castaneda Controversies' (1980)
Jay Courtney Fikes, 'Carlos Castaneda, Academic Opportunism and the Psychedelic Sixties' (1993)
Amy Wallace, 'Sorcerer's Apprentice: My Life with Carlos Castaneda' (2003)
